text:
Safety  is  a  fundamental  requirement  for  au-
tonomous vehicles (AVs) operating in complex ur-
ban environments, where uncertainty arising from
the unpredictable behavior of pedestrians, cyclists,
and other vehicles, combined with dynamic interac-
tions, poses significant challenges. In this context,
safety refers to the ability of a planning approach
to minimize the likelihood of collisions or other
harmful events to an acceptable low threshold, thus
preventing catastrophic consequences. Achieving
1

or Safe LLM-Based Autonomous

such a safe performance is challenging in dynamic
urban environments due to their complexity. Large
Language Models (LLMs) have recently emerged
as powerful tools for generating navigation strate-
gies in autonomous driving, thanks to their “rea-
soning” capabilities over complex traffic scenar-
ios (Chen et al., 2024; Fu et al., 2024; Tian et al.,
2024). However, their tendency to display overcon-
fidence, produce hallucinated outputs, and generate
unreliable uncertainty estimates poses significant
safety risks (Xu et al., 2024; Xiong et al.). Such
vulnerabilities are especially problematic in real-
world domains, where even minor errors can lead
to severe consequences.
To ensure the reliable use of LLMs for path plan-
ning in autonomous driving, we propose SafePath,
the first approach, to the best of our knowledge, to
integrate LLM-based path generation with confor-
mal prediction to provide formal safety guarantees
on the generated paths. SafePath consists of three
stages. First, the Path Generation stage uses an
LLM to produce a set of candidate paths by rea-
soning over the AV goals and environmental cues,
such as traffic rules and agent behaviors. Second,
in the Uncertainty-Aware Path Selection stage,
we refine the set of candidate paths and filter out
risky paths. To achieve this, we introduce a multi-
ple choice question-answering (MCQA) procedure
that leverages a second LLM and conformal pre-
diction (CP) (Shafer and Vovk, 2008), to estimate
uncertainty and guarantee that the refined set of
paths includes, with a user-defined probability, at
least one path that meets safety requirements.
Third, the Path Decision stage determines which
path to execute by examining how similar the paths
in the refined set from the second stage are.  If
the set contains a single path, indicating low un-
certainty, the path is adopted directly.  If the set
contains multiple paths that exhibit high similar-
ity, SafePath selects the one with the lowest ex-
pected collision risk. Only when paths diverge

Motion Planning for Autonomous Navigation.
Motion planning is a central challenge in au-
tonomous driving,  requiring predictive naviga-
tion in dynamic, often congested environments.
Early solutions predominantly relied on rule-
based heuristics that maintained strict adherence
to lane-keeping, safety distances, and traffic reg-
ulations (Ma et al., 2015; Ding et al., 2019; Nils-
son et al., 2015, 2016; Palatti et al., 2021). While
interpretable, these systems struggled to accom-
modate unexpected or extreme scenarios,  limit-
ing their robustness (Wang et al., 2018). More
recent data-driven approaches,  including imita-
tion learning (Pulver et al., 2021; Bronstein et al.,
2022; Cheng et al., 2024) and reinforcement learn-
ing (Li et al., 2021; Osin´ski et al., 2020; Rosbach
et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021),  leverage large-
scale datasets to learn adaptive policies capable
of responding to evolving conditions. However,
their black-box character can impede transparency
and trust, which is critical in safety-sensitive do-
mains (Muhammad et al., 2020).
Large Language Models (LLMs) introduce a
further dimension of explainability,  providing
rationale-based explanations of prospective maneu-
vers (Mao et al., a; Tian et al., 2024; Chen et al.,
2024; Fu et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2025). However,
LLMs may occasionally hallucinate or exhibit un-
warranted overconfidence (Xiong et al.; Xu et al.,
2024), jeopardizing safety if not adequately con-
2

strained. The approach proposed in this work com-
bines LLMs with conformal prediction, preserving
the explainability gains of language-based planning
while mitigating overconfident or incorrect outputs
that could lead to unsafe actions.
Uncertainty Management in Autonomous Driv-
ing. Urban driving involves uncertainties caused
by changing traffic conditions, sensor noise, and in-
complete knowledge of other agent intentions (Tas¸
and Stiller, 2018; Sharma et al., 2020; Yamada
et al., 2023).  Probabilistic approaches based
on Bayesian inference (Michelmore et al., 2020;
Zheng et al., 2024; Caprio et al., 2024) and stochas-
tic formulations,  including POMDPs (Brechtel
et al., 2014), model these uncertainties but can
be computationally heavy and rely on assumptions
about underlying data distributions. Redundancy
and ensemble-based strategies (Tang et al., 2022),
as well as uncertainty-aware reinforcement learn-
ing (Diehl et al., 2023; Rezaee et al., 2021; Hsu
et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2021), offer additional
means of handling unpredictable environments but
often lack formal statistical guarantees.
Conformal prediction avoids strict distributional
assumptions and provides statistically valid confi-
dence bounds on its predictions (Lindemann et al.,
2023; Lekeufack et al., 2024), making it promising
for safety-critical planning. Recent efforts to inte-
grate conformal prediction with LLMs (Ren et al.;
Wang et al., 2024, 2023; Liang et al., 2024; Sun
et al., 2024) show its potential for improving relia-
bility. In this work, We build on these insights by
leveraging conformal prediction to mitigate LLM
overconfidence, yielding motion plans with explicit
safety guarantees in the face of uncertainty.

Conformal prediction is a statistical framework that
transforms the outputs of a deep learning model
into prediction sets guaranteed to contain the true
outcome with a probability 1 − α, where α is user-
defined error rate (Vovk et al., 2005; Lekeufack
et al., 2024). Unlike point estimates, the prediction
sets explicitly quantify uncertainty by including all
plausible candidates, making CP ideal for safety-
critical systems where overconfidence must be mit-
igated. While we later apply CP to the selection
of safe paths in autonomous navigation, we first
introduce its general form here.
Constructing Conformal Prediction Sets. Let X
be the input space and Y the label space. Suppose

4  Approach
4.1  Approach Overview
SafePath ensures the safe use of LLMs in au-
tonomous path planning by providing formal safety
guarantees on the output of the LLM-generated
paths. As illustrated in Figure 1, SafePath con-
sists of three sequential stages: Path Generation,
Uncertainty-Aware Path Selection, and Path Deci-
sion.
In the Path Generation stage, the AV employs
a first language model, LLM1, to generate a di-
verse set of candidate paths based on its mission
goals and the current environmental state, which in-
cludes perception data and predicted movements of
surrounding agents. Each candidate path is accom-
panied by a rationale, enhancing interpretability
and transparency in the decision-making process.
The Uncertainty-Aware Path Selection stage
refines the generated set of paths to address the

inherent variability and uncertainty in LLM out-
puts. This refinement process employs a second
LLM, LLM2, to select paths from the generated
candidates. We frame this selection as a multiple
choice question-answering task, where LLM2 re-
ceives the relevant scenario context and chooses
the most suitable path among the candidates. The
structured and bounded MCQA formulation en-
ables us to apply conformal prediction to calibrate
the selection process and account for uncertainty.
After conformal prediction, the output of this stage
is a prediction set of paths that is guaranteed to
include at least one path that meets the required
safety criteria, with a user-defined probability.
In the Path Decision stage, the autonomous vehi-
cle (AV) evaluates the prediction set obtained from
the previous stage to determine the final decision
for execution. If the set contains a single path, the
AV adopts it immediately. If the set contains multi-
ple paths that are sufficiently similar—indicating
a consensus among safe trajectories—the AV will
autonomously proceed using the path deemed to
have the lowest expected collision risk. In scenar-
ios where the set contains multiple diverging paths,
reflecting a high degree of uncertainty, the system
defers to human oversight to resolve the ambiguity.
4.2  Path Generation
In the Path Generation stage,  the AV employs
a fine-tuned LLM that utilizes chain-of-thought
reasoning (CoT) (Wei et al., 2022) to generate a
set of candidate paths based on its current state,
perception data, and mission goals.  Let x =
{xpercept, xpred, xgoal} represent the AV’s percep-
tion data, predicted movements of other agents,
and mission objectives. By leveraging chain-of-
thought reasoning, the LLM explores various pos-
sible trajectories, considering the AV’s goals and
the behavior of surrounding agents.
Formally, the LLMgenerates a set of k candidate
paths P = {p1, p2, . . . , pk} and their associated
rationales R = {r1, r2, . . . , rk} through chain-of-
thought reasoning:
k
{(pi, ri)}i=1 = LLM1(x)  (4)
Each path pi represents a potential trajectory for
the AV, while ri provides the detailed reasoning
process that led to the selection of that path. The
CoT rationale enhances transparency and can be
used for further analysis or to explain decisions to
human operators. Furthermore, several works, such
4

as (Chen et al., 2024; Fu et al., 2024; Wei et al.,
2022), have demonstrated that generating rationales
alongside the output leads to more reliable and
interpretable results. We provide the full text of the
prompts we use to generate the candidate path in
Appendix A.6.
4.3  Uncertainty-Aware Path Selection
Despite fine-tuning, LLM1, may generate unsafe
paths due to overconfident or hallucinated out-
puts (Gekhman et al., 2024). To address this limi-
tation, SafePath integrates conformal prediction to
provide statistical guarantees that the selected path
set contains at least one safe trajectory with prob-
ability 1 − α. However, a critical challenge arises
from the unstructured nature of LLM-generated
paths, which vary in semantic detail and lack a
predefined hypothesis space, both essential for con-
formal prediction guarantees.

We first formalize
what we mean by a safe path:
Definition 4.1 (Safe Path) A path p is considered
safe if it satisfies the following constraints:
Collision Avoidance: p ensures no intersections
with other agents or static obstacles in the environ-
ment.
Rule Compliance: p adheres to all traffic laws and
operational constraints.
Safety Margins: p maintains a safe distance from
other agents, given their predicted trajectories.
Let Sx denote the set of safe paths given a scenario
x. For our calibrations and guarantees to hold, we
assume:
Assumption 4.1 The driving datasets used to train
and calibrate the planning models contain only safe
paths.
Assumption 4.1 ensures that the models and calibra-
tion processes are based on safe driving behaviors,
which is reasonable given that training data for au-
tonomous vehicles are curated to reflect correct and
safe operations and used to train models that do so.
To reconcile unstructured LLM outputs with
conformal prediction’s requirements, we reformu-
late path selection as a multiple-choice question-
answering (MCQA) task.  Each candidate path
pi ∈ P is assigned a distinct label lpi (e.g., “A,”
“B,” “C”). Then we prompt a second LLM, LLM2
to select a single letter corresponding to the most
∗
suitable path l  , given the scenario description x,
the set of paths P , and their rationale R:
∗
l = LLM2(P, R, x).  (5)

Casting path selection as a multiple choice task
restricts the response space of LLM2 to a finite set
of options, which enables the computation of logit-
based probabilities even for closed-source LLMs
through their APIs.  By extracting the logits,  it
is possible to compute non-conformity scores to
construct statistically valid prediction sets. Under
Assumption 4.1, we now define a non-conformity
score spi  for each path pi using the Least Am-
biguous Classification (LAC) score (Sadinle et al.,
1

spi for every candidate path pi, and collect all paths
whose scores do not exceed qˆ:
C = { pi ∈ P : spi ≤ qˆ}.
This prediction set C contains, with probability at
least 1 − α, a correct (and thus safe) path for the
new scenario. Concretely, we have:
Theorem 4.1 (Prediction Set Safety Guarantee)
Let C ⊆ P be the prediction set generated above at
confidence level 1−α, and suppose Assumption 4.1
holds. Then,

Theorem 4.1 states that the prediction set C pro-
duced by conformal prediction will, with high prob-
ability, contain at least one path that meets the
safety requirements of Definition 4.1. We prove
Theorem 4.1 by leveraging the statistical guaran-
tees of conformal prediction, which ensures that
the prediction set includes the true path with prob-
ability at least 1 − α and datasets are assumed to
contain safe paths. The detailed proof is provided
in Appendix A.1.

2020). However, as we show in Section 5.4, LAC yielded the
best results.

4.4  Path Decision
Once we have a prediction set C of candidate paths,
we determine which path to execute. If C contains
a single path, i.e., |C| = 1, the AV adopts it imme-
diately since it is included with probability 1 − α
of being safe. Otherwise, we evaluate how similar
the paths in C are to each other.
Definition 4.2 (δ-Equivalence) Two  paths
pi, pj ∈ P are δ-equivalent if their similarity, as
measured by a function Sim : P × P → [0, 1],
satisfies:
Sim(pi, pj) ≥ δ,  (9)
where δ ∈ [0, 1] is a predefined threshold. This
ensures that paths with small deviations, both in
geometry and operational characteristics, are con-
sidered interchangeable in terms of safety and func-
2
tionality  .
If all pairs of paths in C are δ-equivalent (i.e., the
set C effectively contains similar paths), the AV
selects the path with the lowest expected collision
based on predictions in the visual scene description.
We define Collision(p) as the fraction of agents
with which p intersects out of the total number of
agents:
#{agents collided with p}

Hence, the final path is chosen according to
pfinal = argminCollision(p).  (11)
p∈C
If, on the other hand, there exists at least one pair
(pi, pj) with Sim(pi, pj) < δ,  the AV defers to
a human operator to avoid committing to highly
divergent or conflicting trajectories.

In Definition 4.2, Sim is defined as Sim : P ×P → [0, 1].
In Algorithm 1, we extend this notation to sets, implying that
similarity is computed pairwise for all path pairs..

To empirically validate SafePath, we structure our
evaluation around three core goals: (1) understand-
ing how delegation can be adaptively controlled,
(2) assessing the statistical validity and efficiency
of our conformal prediction layer, and (3) bench-
marking overall planning safety in diverse settings.
We begin by examining the effect of the path-
similarity threshold δ, which governs human dele-
gation and autonomy. Next, we evaluate SafePath’s
coverage performance across different confidence
levels (α) using conformal prediction, and com-
pare it to state-of-the-art conformal methods. Fi-
nally, we assess collision-avoidance performance
in both open-loop and closed-loop settings: open-
loop tests SafePath’s reasoning from static inputs,
while closed-loop evaluates SafePath in interactive
scenarios where decisions influence future states.
Additional results are in the Appendix, including
additional metrics (App. A.2), aggregation strate-
gies (App. A.3), other LLMs (App. A.4), and qual-
itative examples (App. A.5).
5.1  Dataset and Simulation Environments
We evaluate SafePath using two complementary
testbeds covering distinct planning modes.  For
open-loop evaluation, we use nuScenes (Caesar
et al., 2020), a real-world dataset comprising 1,000
driving scenes, and over 40,000 annotated frames.
For  closed-loop  evaluation,  we  use
highway-env  (Leurent,  2018),  a  simulation
environment  that  supports  interactive  agent
control.  It includes scenarios such as highways,
intersections, and roundabouts, allowing SafePath
to be tested in settings where each decision affects
future states.
5.2  Implementation
We use gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 for both path gener-
ation (LLM1) and path selection (LLM2). LLM1 is
fine-tuned on nuScenes for one epoch (cost: $120
via OpenAI API), while LLM2 remains frozen.
Both LLMs use system prompts with few-shot ex-
amples (Appendix A.6).
Conformal prediction is calibrated using 5,000
held-out training samples. Path similarity is com-

5.3  Effects of Path Similarity Threshold δ
We analyze how the similarity threshold δ affects
human delegation (HuD). Higher δ enforces stricter
agreement among paths, increasing intervention
when candidates differ. Figure 2 shows HuD rates
across α ∈ 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, where α is the con-
formal prediction error tolerance.
As δ increases, SafePath requires greater path
similarity, leading to more frequent human inter-
vention.  At α = 0.10,  increasing δ from 0.85
to 0.95 raises HuD from 0.00 to 0.35, indicating
stricter caution. Yet, HuD remains bounded, and
autonomy is preserved at or below δ = 0.85. No-
tably, δ has a stronger impact on HuD than α; for
example, at δ = 0.95, raising α from 0.10 to 0.30
only reduces HuD from 0.35 to 0.32.
Unless otherwise stated, we set δ = 0.85 to
ensure HuD = 0, enabling fully autonomous oper-
ation in all remaining experiments.
5.4  Conformal Prediction Evaluation
We evaluate the conformal prediction step to as-
sess SafePath’s ability to construct prediction sets
that reliably include the correct path. For this ex-
periment only, the ground-truth trajectory is added
to the candidate set to directly measure coverage.
Evaluation is performed across confidence levels
(α) using δ = 0.85 (no human delegation).
Baselines.  We compare SafePath against adap-
tive predictive sets (APS) (Romano et al., 2020),
7

which guarantees conditional coverage, regular-
ized adaptive predictive sets (RAPS) (Angelopou-
los et al., 2021), which aims to reduce set sizes, and
“knowNo” (Ren et al.), a planning framework with
conformal prediction and human delegation. We
run experiments at multiple α values, correspond-
ing to different target coverage rates 1 − α.
Metrics.  In this evaluation we use the following
metrics that are proper for conformal prediction.
• Deviation from Target Coverage (DTC). The
deviation from the target coverage rate (DTC)
quantifies the discrepancy between the achieved
coverage, Covapproach, and the desired target
coverage, Covtarget = 1 − α.
• Set Size (SS). This metric represents the average
set size of the prediction sets. The higher the
cardinality of the set, the higher the uncertainty.
• Human Delegation Rate (HuD). This metric
represents the rate of cases that are delegated to
humans in case of high uncertainty.
Results Analysis.  Table 1 shows that SafePath
achieves consistent performance across all confi-
dence levels (α).

Its path decision stage reduces
the effective prediction set size (SS) to 1.00 by
selecting the path with the lowest expected colli-
sion risk when candidates are sufficiently similar,
eliminating the need for human intervention.
In terms of Deviation from Target Coverage
(DTC), SafePath remains close to zero and on par
with APS and RAPS. At α = 0.2, for example,
SafePath yields a DTC of −0.03, compared to
−0.08 for APS and −0.05 for RAPS, confirming
reliable coverage with the specified confidence.
SafePath also has a clear advantage in Human
Delegation rate (HuD): with δ = 0.85, it achieves
HuD = 0.00 at all α values, ensuring fully au-
tonomous operation.  In contrast, KnowNo dele-
gates 100% of decisions, as it lacks a mechanism
to control delegation frequency under uncertainty.
While APS and RAPS maintain low DTC, their
prediction sets are larger (SS ≈ 2.6–4.5), reflect-
ing higher uncertainty and no internal aggregation
strategy. SafePath’s δ-equivalence mechanism ef-
fectively consolidates paths into a single decision,
reducing uncertainty by 77%.
5.5  Comparison with State-of-the-Art
We evaluate SafePath on the nuScenes benchmark,
using collision rate as the primary metric.  Ad-.

This work introduced SafePath, an approach for
safety-centric path planning under uncertainty in
autonomous driving, by combining LLMs, confor-
mal prediction, and selective human intervention.
SafePath provides formal safety guarantees and
demonstrated significant improvements in predic-
tion uncertainty, collision rates, and reduced human
intervention compared to state-of-the-art methods.

While SafePath demonstrates strong performance
on large-scale datasets, such as nuScenes and simu-
lators such as Highway-env, real-world deployment
remains a critical next step. Our results provide
a solid foundation, but further validation in con-
trolled environments is essential to evaluate both
performance and user acceptance. Controlled ex-
periments, such as those conducted on test tracks
or in virtual reality setups (Doula et al., 2023), can
simulate diverse real-world scenarios, enabling a
rigorous assessment of SafePath’s applicability and
reliability. The reliance on LLMs for both path
generation and selection introduces computational
overhead, raising concerns about real-time feasi-
bility.  In our current implementation, we relied
on APIs, which reduces computational overhead
and provides an interface with closed-source LLM
such as gpt − 4.o, and on lower precision to per-
form tests with local LLMs, as we show in Ap-
pendix A.4. Furthermore, the theoretical guaran-
tees provided by CP rely on the exchangeability
assumption and the representativeness of the cali-
bration dataset (Vovk et al., 2005; Lekeufack et al.,
2024). While the nuScenes dataset covers challeng-
ing scenarios, extending the approach to handle
out-of-distribution data (OOD), will enhance its
robustness. SafePath is flexible in those terms and
is not constrained to a single non-conformity score,
making it adaptive to scoring functions that handle
OOD (Gibbs and Candes, 2021; Kaur et al., 2022).

A Appendix
A.1  Proofs
A.1.1  Proof for Theorem 4.1
Proof A.1 Conformal prediction constructs predic-
tion sets that contain the true output with a spec-
ified probability 1 − α, under the assumption of
exchangeability between the calibration data and
the test data. In our context, the "true output" refers
to a safe path. By calibrating the confidence scores
of the second LLM using a dataset of known safe
paths, we ensure that the prediction set C includes
the correct (safe) path with probability at least
1 − α. Therefore, the probability that C contains
at least one safe path is at least 1 − α.
A.1.2  Proof for Theorem 4.2
Proof A.2 From Theorem 4.1, the prediction set C
contains at least one safe path with probability at
least 1 − α.
The Path Decision stage proceeds as follows:
Case 1: If |C| = 1, the single path p in C is se-
lected for execution. Since p is in C, and C con-
tains at least one safe path with probability at least
1 − α, the selected path is safe with the same prob-
ability.
Case 2:  If |C| > 1 and the paths in C are δ-
equivalent (i.e., Sim(pi, pj) ≥ δ for all pi, pj ∈
C), the approach aggregates these paths to produce
a final path pfinal. Given that at least one path in C
is safe (from Theorem 4.1) and the paths are highly
similar, the sampled path pfinal will also be safe
with probability at least 1 − α. This is because the
sampling from δ-equivalent paths that are close in
terms of safety characteristics preserves the safety
properties.
Case 3: If the paths in C are not δ-equivalent, in-
dicating significant divergence among candidate
paths or if C is an empty set, the approach dele-
gates the decision to a human operator. Human
intervention is assumed to result in a safe action, as
1

the operator can use additional context and judg-
ment not available to the autonomous system.
In all cases, the SafePath approach ensures that
the path executed by the AV is safe with probability
at least 1 − α. The combination of statistical guar-
antees from conformal prediction and the struc-
tured decision-making process in the Path Decision
stage provides robust safety assurances.
A.2 More Results Using L2 Metric
The L2 metric evaluates the distance between the
planned and ground-truth trajectories. While L2
distance is commonly used as a performance met-
ric in open-loop predictions, it is important to note
that it does not serve as a definitive indicator of
the effectiveness of a planning strategy (Zhai et al.,
2023). This limitation arises from two primary rea-
sons. First, the planned trajectory may differ from
the trajectory of the ego-vehicle used to record the
dataset, even if both are valid. Second, studies have
shown that simple two-layer fully connected neural
networks can accurately predict trajectory distribu-
tions using only the ego-vehicle’s past paths, with-
out incorporating information about the surround-
ing environment (Zhai et al., 2023). This reduces
the metric’s ability to meaningfully capture the
planner’s capability to consider and react to com-
plex external factors. For completeness, we report
the results of SafePath on the L2 metric in Table 4.
The reported results are obtained using α=0.50 and
δ=0.90 leading to HuD = 0%. while maintain-
ing competitive trajectory accuracy.  In terms of
trajectory accuracy measured by the L2 distance,
SafePath has an average L2 error of 0.84 meters.
While this is higher than the lowest L2 errors re-
ported by methods such as DriveVLM (Tian et al.,
2024) (0.40m) and VLP-VAD (Jiang et al., 2023)
(0.50m), it is comparable to GPT-Driver (Mao et al.,
b) (0.84m) and better than earlier methods like
FF (Hu et al., 2021) (1.43m). This suggests that
SafePath maintains acceptable trajectory accuracy
while emphasizing safety.

various path decision strategies when the predic-
tion set contains multiple δ-equivalent paths. The
strategies examined are:

2. Average: Computing the pointwise average

3. Weighted Average:  Averaging the paths

4. Best Path: Selecting the path with the high-

5. Minimum Collision Sampling: Choosing
the path with the minimum expected collision
probability based on input prompts.
Tables 5 and 6 present the results of these strate-
gies at (α = 0.3, δ = 0.85) and (α = 0.5, δ =
0.85), respectively, reporting the L2 trajectory er-
ror and collision rates at 1 s, 2 s, and 3 s horizons.
The Average and Weighted Average strategies
consistently achieve the lowest L2 trajectory errors
across both settings of α. Specifically, these strate-
gies yield average L2 errors of 0.71m and 0.72m
at α = 0.3 and α = 0.5, respectively. This sug-
gests that aggregating paths, whether equally or
weighted by confidence scores, leads to more accu-
rate trajectory predictions. The minimal difference
between the Average and Weighted Average strate-
gies implies that the softmax confidence scores do

The Random Sampling and Best Path strategies
exhibit higher L2 errors and collision rates com-
pared to the averaging strategies and Minimum
Collision Sampling. The Best Path strategy, which
selects the path with the highest softmax probabil-
ity, does not perform as well as anticipated. This
could be due to calibration issues with the softmax
scores or a weak correlation between the confi-
dence scores and actual collision risk. These find-
ings raise concerns about the reliability of using
softmax probabilities as indicators of path safety
and highlight the need for better calibration of these
confidence measures.

trajectory accuracy and collision avoidance. The

synthesizing information from multiple paths but
may not minimize collision risk as effectively as
strategies that explicitly consider collision probabil-
ities. Conversely, theMinimumCollision Sampling
strategy enhances safety by reducing collisions but
may compromise on trajectory precision.
It is important to consider that while averaging
paths can produce smoother trajectories, this ap-
proach might inadvertently smooth out necessary
evasive maneuvers that individual paths would have
taken to avoid obstacles, potentially increasing col-
lision risk. Therefore, careful evaluation of how
path aggregation impacts safety-critical behaviors
is essential.
Overall, the results indicate that no single strat-
egy universally outperforms the others across all
metrics.

The selection of an appropriate path de-
cision strategy depends on the specific priorities
and constraints of the autonomous navigation task.
For applications where safety and collision avoid-
ance are highly important, strategies like Minimum
Collision Sampling are preferable.
A.4  Effect of LLMModels on the Conformal
Prediction Performance
We evaluate the performance of SafePath with dif-
ferent LLMs at the Uncertainty Aware Path Selec-
tion stage. LLMs can be biased when it comes to
MCQA (Ren et al.), which can limit the effects of
the conformal prediction operation, especially if
the calibration set is limited. So it is crucial to in-
vestigate the performance of several LLMs to guide
future research.

falcon − 7B,  and llama3.1 − 7B − instruct.

GPT − 4.o consistently achieves the most stable

DTC values that remain within a small range across

sets, particularly at higher α levels, underscores its
efficiency in balancing coverage and set size.  In
contrast, falcon − 7B displays variability in DTC,
transitioning from under- to over-coverage as α
increases, with a fixed SS that indicates limited
adaptability. llama3.1 − 7B − instruct shows
improvement in DTC with increasing α, though its
larger SS values at lower α levels suggest ineffi-
ciencies in stricter confidence scenarios.
Overall, GPT − 4.o emerges as the most  ro-
bust model, demonstrating strong calibration and
efficient prediction set  sizing.  The observed
variability in falcon − 7B and inefficiencies in
llama3.1 − 7B − instruct highlight opportuni-
ties for refining their calibration strategies. These
results emphasize the importance of selecting
and optimizing LLMs for conformal prediction
in applications where accuracy and efficiency are
paramount.

A.6  Prompts
In the following, we describe the prompts used
to fine-tune and query the large language mod-
els (LLMs) for trajectory planning and decision-
making in the context of autonomous vehicles.
Each figure illustrates a specific prompt designed
to guide the LLMs through distinct stages of the
task, ensuring consistency, safety, and adaptability
in path generation and selection under uncertainty.
Figure 6 presents the systemmessage used in the
trajectory generation phase. The prompt defines
the role of the model as a planner tasked with gener-
ating four unique, collision-free trajectories over a
3-second horizon. It specifies the inputs, including
perception data, historical trajectory, ego-vehicle
states, and the mission goal, as well as the expected
structure of the output, which includes reasoning,
meta-actions, and the planned trajectories.

vehicle will follow given the Mission Goal. Consider reasoning to
make a choice.
